exemplar.title:
Using calculus and kinematics to find the optimum trajectory that a
handball long-range shot should take considering the angle of release to make it easier for handball players to score maximum long-range goals at different distances from the goal without having to struggle.

detailedExemplarData.title:
Using calculus and kinematics to find the optimum trajectory that a
handball long-range shot should take considering the angle of release to make it easier for handball players to score maximum long-range goals at different distances from the goal without having to struggle.

subject.title: Mathematics Analysis and Approaches (AA)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics Analysis and Approaches** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **number and algebra, functions, geometry and trigonometry, statistics and probability, and calculus**, with a strong focus on **theoretical understanding, formal mathematical reasoning, and analytical problem-solving**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Non-calculator written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 2** | Calculator allowed written exam | 90 minutes | 120 minutes | 40% |
| **Paper 3** | Calculator allowed problem-solving | N/A | 60 minutes | 20% (HL only) |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal Assessment | 20 hours | 20 hours | 20% |

strengths: Clear overall structure with recognizable introduction, method, analysis and conclusion
Major headings and numbered figures/tables allow the reader to navigate the exploration
Logical development usually links purpose, methodology and findings
weaknesses: Occasional jumps in reasoning (e.g. switch of angle assumptions) break flow
Inconsistent subsection numbering and missing page references in the table of contents
Repetitive background material and digressions reduce conciseness
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strand assessments place this work in Band 3 for coherence and organization, with mostly relevant content. Despite clear signposting and a sensible order, lapses in numbering and verbosity prevent a topâband score, justifying a 3/4.

strengths: Effective use of multiple representations (tables, two scatter graphs, schematic diagrams) to support the argument
Standard symbols (v, g, Î¸, Hâââ) and basic calculus notation used appropriately in most places
Graphical and tabular forms enhance clarity of data trends
weaknesses: Inconsistent notation (units missing or confused, degree/radian symbols fluctuate)
Derivations sometimes jump steps without clear explanation
Axis labels and scales are omitted in key schematics, hindering interpretation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student demonstrates effective use of different representations and mostly consistent mathematical language, aligning with a Band 3 performance. Notation lapses and occasional clarity issues preclude Band 4, so a 3/4 is awarded.

strengths: Investigation grounded in the studentâs own handball experience, showing genuine engagement
Clear personal approach in choosing angles and court distances
Evidence of independent thinking in framing the sporting question
weaknesses: Modelling closely follows standard projectile formulae with little novel technique
Creativity limited to typical textbook applications
Initiative beyond basic setup is only suggested in extensions rather than fully explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Strands for independent thinking and personal approach sit in Band 2, while creativity is limited. This warrants a solid 2/3, reflecting significant but not exceptional personal engagement.

strengths: Acknowledges model limitations (e.g. Magnus effect, 3D constraints) with thoughtful comment
Proposes meaningful extensions (3D simulations, optimization by differentiation)
Includes some critical comments on measurement challenges
weaknesses: Reflection on results is basic and lacks quantitative support (no correlation coefficients)
Evaluation of outcomes is general, without tying recommendations directly to data
Critical analysis is not sustained throughout the exploration
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: There is more than minimal reflectionâlimitations and extensions show critical engagementâbut analysis remains at a surface level. This fits Band 2, meriting 2/3.

strengths: Relevant projectileâmotion equations and vertex form of a quadratic are applied appropriately
Mathematics is commensurate with SL syllabus level
Overall understanding is good, with correct setup of trigonometric and algebraic models
weaknesses: Algebraic simplification errors (missing constant terms) lead to implausible height values
Unit slips and a typographical âqâ instead of âgâ indicate minor inaccuracies
Some numerical results defy physical intuition and require re-evaluation
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work uses relevant mathematics effectively and at the correct syllabus level, showing good understanding with mostly minor errors. These characteristics match Band 4, so a 4/6 is awarded.

strandSelections.reasoning: Within sections, lines of reasoning are occasionally unclearâe.g. the derivation of the quadratic model jumps between vertex and factored forms without explicit justification, and the rationale for translating the curve by 2 m is asserted rather than explained. These issues indicate âsome logical structure and clarityâ rather than the consistently clear exposition required for a higher band.

Calculations generally follow correct formulae, yet several numerical results are implausible (e.g. a 25 m horizontal shot yielding a 25 m vertical height). Unit slips and an erroneous denominator âqâ show incomplete understanding. These substantive errors indicate âsome understanding with errorsâ rather than the higher-band expectations of mostly correct work.

The student employs algebraic working, tables of calculated values, two scatter graphs, and schematic diagrams of the handball court and projectile. While the quality of the graphs is basic, the combination of symbolic, tabular and graphical forms supports the mathematical argument effectively, meeting the âeffective use of different formsâ descriptor.

The student structures the investigation with a recognisable introduction, background, method, analysis and conclusion. Ideas generally follow one another in a sensible order, allowing the reader to trace the purpose, methodology and findings. Although some jumps in reasoning occur (e.g. the sudden change from a specific 45Â° assumption to recommending 30Â°), the thread of the argument is seldom lost. This level of linkage aligns with the descriptor of a coherent, logically developed exploration rather than an âexcellentâ one.

Some creativity is shown in adapting projectile equations to a handball context and in constructing simple graphs, yet the modelling remains rudimentary and largely derivative of standard examples; initiative such as empirical testing or optimisation via calculus is suggested only in âExtensionsâ. Hence the evidence corresponds to âsome creativity or initiativeâ.

The student notes that higher trajectories give goalkeepers more reaction time and refers to positive correlations, but provides only surface-level discussion; no quantitative assessment (e.g. correlation coefficient, model fit) or critical appraisal of error sources is offered. This constitutes âbasic analysisâ.

Outcome evaluation is confined to generalised advice (use 45Â° beyond 25 m, 30Â° closer) without testing against real data or estimating reliability. Future work suggestions are generic. Such treatment is consistent with âlimited evaluation of outcomesâ.

Reflection is limited to brief remarks on measurement difficulty and oversimplification. These comments are isolated rather than woven through the work, matching the descriptor of âlimited reflectionâ rather than âmeaningful reflection throughoutâ.

Standard symbols (v, g, Î¸, H_max) are used and basic calculus notation appears, yet units are missing or confused in places (m/s written as m/s2; g re-introduced with different symbols). Explanations around sine notation and degree/radian conversions show lapses. These inconsistencies fit âsome appropriate language and notationâ rather than âmostly consistentâ usage.

The investigation is framed around a personal sporting question and decisions (e.g. the choice of 45Â° then reconsideration of 30Â°) reflect the studentâs own line of inquiry, demonstrating a clear personal approach. Nonetheless, implementation mirrors textbook procedure, so the approach cannot be described as âhighly personal and innovativeâ.

Most content relates to the stated aim of optimising a handball trajectory, but repetition of textbook definitions, lengthy quotation of equations already listed, and digressions on playing positions add superfluous bulk. Wordiness in the conclusion and background reduces concision, so the work is âmostly relevantâ rather than consistently concise.

The mathematics (projectile-motion equations, vertex form of a quadratic, basic kinematics) is directly relevant to modelling a handball shot and is applied correctly in most cases. Although the scenario simplifies reality, chosen mathematics is broadly appropriate and purposeful, satisfying the descriptor for band 3.

Selecting a personally relevant context (the studentâs experience as a handball player) and collecting data points at chosen court distances shows significant independent thinking. However, the mathematics stays close to standard projectile formulae; no novel modelling techniques are introduced, preventing placement in the top band.

A table of contents is provided and major headings (Introduction, Background, Data Collection, etc.) are clearly sign-posted. Figures and tables are numbered, and the reader can locate information without difficulty. Nonetheless, subsections are sometimes mislabeled (Roman numerals/I, II, Ð¨) and pagination or cross-referencing is inconsistent, preventing a seamless flow expected in the top band.

The work employs algebra, trigonometric functions and graphing that sit comfortably within the SL syllabus. There is no material extending beyond SL, but the mathematics used is solidly commensurate with course level, warranting placement in band 3.

annotations.comment: Definitions of trajectory, projectile motion and kinematics are brief and lack connection to handball context. Enhance relevance by linking definitions directly to the parameters of the shot under study.
The schematic of multiple shot trajectories lacks axis labels and scale markings, making interpretation of comparative angles and distances difficult. Label axes clearly.
The kinematics table presents comprehensive equations, but units for g are inconsistent. Ensure $$g=9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$$ is stated uniformly to maintain precision.
Assuming zeros at (0,0) and (10,0) for the parabola requires justification in projectile context; clarify why the crossbar and launch height coincide at ground level.
The steps for completing the square (Equation 2) merge multiple lines without clear separation, causing confusion. Break derivation into distinct stages and explain each algebraic move.
The conversion from vertex to standard form omits the constant term resulting from expansion of $$-0.1448(x-5)^2$$; recheck algebraic simplification to ensure accuracy.
The student effectively grounds the investigation in personal sporting experience, demonstrating clear engagement by relating mathematical modelling to their own handball playing context.

This introductory paragraph is overly verbose and digresses into playing positions rather than focusing concisely on the mathematical problem. Consider trimming background detail and maintaining relevance to the aim.
The aim statement lacks specificity about variables to be determined or ranges considered. Clarify parameters (e.g., release angle range, initial speed) to strengthen mathematical focus.
The court diagram (Figure 1) is clear, accurately labelled and effectively supports spatial understanding of distances used in modelling.
Notation for the release angle fluctuates between â45^0â and â45^{\circ}â. Consistently use correct LaTeX notation $$^{\circ}$$ and define angle units to avoid ambiguity.
The vertical translation rationale is only asserted; explain mathematically why adding 2 shifts the launch height correctly rather than simply stating the step.
The use of GDC to compute initial velocity is noted but no manual working is provided. Include intermediate steps or a worked example to validate calculator results.
In the maximum height formula for 20 m (TextInlineMath), denominator appears as \(2q\) instead of \(2g\), indicating a typographical error that must be corrected.
Figure 7 visually supports the positive correlation between maximum height and time taken, offering an effective representation of data trends.
The analysis of the heightâtime correlation notes imprecision but lacks quantitative support (e.g., correlation coefficient). Include numerical assessment to substantiate claims.
The conclusion recommends a 30Â° release for shorter distances without demonstrating how the data support this specific angle. Tie recommendations directly to quantitative findings.
The challenges section briefly notes measurement and model oversimplification but could be expanded with deeper critical reflection on error sources and their impact.
The limitations section thoughtfully acknowledges factors such as Magnus effect and 3D trajectory constraints, showing insightful awareness of model boundaries.
Assumptions are listed comprehensively but lack justification for why certain simplifications (e.g., neglecting spin) are acceptable. Provide rationale for each assumptionâs validity.
Extensions propose use of 3D simulations and optimization by differentiation, demonstrating initiative in broadening the mathematical scope of the investigation.
Maximum heights for 25 m and 30 m distances exceed horizontal range (e.g., 35.21 m height for 30 m shot). Re-evaluate calculations as these results are physically implausible.
The second scatter plot omits axis titles and units, hindering clarity in interpreting the relationship between height and time. Always label axes and include measurement units.
Table of contents uses inconsistent numbering (I, Ð, Ð¨) and omits page references for several sections. Standardize numbering and include page numbers for easy navigation.
The continuation of the table of contents omits entries for VI and VII on the same page, hindering orientation. Include all sections with aligned formatting.
The derivation of time-to-peak in later sections uses inconsistent variable names (sometimes \(a\) instead of \(g\)). Maintain uniform naming to avoid confusion.

similarExemplars.title: How does the angle of release of a basketball shot from the wrist analyzed using trigonometric principles, unit circle modeling, statistical data, parabolic trajectory analysis, and calculus support the hypothesis that close to 45 degrees angle is the most optimal for maximizing shot efficiency in terms of vertical height and horizontal displacement?
